2016 · ICLR 2017 · Post-training & RL

Categorical Reparameterization with Gumbel-Softmax

Add Gumbel noise to a categorical's logits and take a softmax at temperature τ — a differentiable proxy for a discrete sample that turns backprop through categorical choices into ordinary reparameterization.

Eric Jang, Shixiang Gu, Ben Poole
venue ICLR 2017 citations 6,510 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Stochastic neural networks with discrete latent variables are the natural way to represent a lot of structure in the world: a semantic class, a discrete memory address, an attended-to image region, a discrete action in reinforcement learning. The trouble is backpropagation — while it computes parameter gradients efficiently through any deterministic, differentiable layer — simply cannot be run through a non-differentiable sampling step. You can't ask "how does nudging θ change this one-hot draw" when the draw is a step function of θ's randomness.

Two families of workaround existed before this paper, and both have real costs. The first is score-function estimators (REINFORCE and its variance-reduced descendants — NVIL, DARN, MuProp, VIMCO): these give an unbiased gradient via the log-derivative trick and never need to differentiate through the sample itself, but their variance scales linearly with the dimensionality of the sample vector, which is exactly what makes them painful for categorical variables with more than a couple of classes. The second is biased path-derivative estimators such as the Straight-Through (ST) estimator, which was built specifically for Bernoulli variables and approximates the non-existent gradient as 1 (or as the sample-independent mean). Neither family was formulated with categorical variables as the first-class target. The paper's stated contribution is to close exactly that gap: a reparameterizable continuous relaxation built for categorical variables, so that a discrete choice sitting inside a differentiable pipeline (a VAE's discrete latent code, an agent's discrete action, a discriminative label) can be trained with ordinary backpropagation instead of a bespoke, high-variance gradient estimator.

// how it works

The method

Start from the Gumbel-Max trick (Gumbel, 1954; Maddison et al., 2014), a well-known way to draw an exact sample from a categorical distribution with class probabilities π: add i.i.d. Gumbel(0,1) noise gᵢ to each class's log-probability and take the arg max. That's a perfectly valid sampler, but arg max has a zero gradient almost everywhere — it's a hard, non-differentiable operation. The paper's move is to swap arg max for a softmax at temperature τ, which is a smooth, differentiable relaxation of the same operation:

yi = exp((log πi + gi) / τ)Σj exp((log πj + gj) / τ)  for i = 1, …, k // the Gumbel-Softmax distribution — a continuous density over the simplex Δᵏ⁻¹ that this paper derives in closed form (their Eq. 3)

This is what makes it a reparameterization trick in the same family as the VAE's z = μ + σ·N(0,1): the randomness (gᵢ) is pushed into an independent noise source that doesn't depend on the parameters, so the sample y becomes a deterministic, differentiable function of π and that noise, and ∂y/∂π is well-defined. As τ→0, the softmax sharpens toward the arg max — samples become one-hot and the Gumbel-Softmax distribution converges to the true categorical p(z). As τ→∞, the distribution flattens toward uniform. Figure 1 below shows exactly this interpolation, and it's the crux of the method's central tension: at low temperature, samples are close to genuinely discrete (low bias) but the softmax's gradient gets steep and noisy (high variance); at high temperature, gradients are smooth and low-variance, but the sample is far from anything the downstream model will actually see at inference (the categorical class it's meant to approximate). There's no free setting of τ — every use of this estimator inherits that trade-off, which the paper addresses in practice by starting at a high temperature and annealing down over training.

Gumbel-Softmax samples are continuous points on the simplex — useful when the downstream computation can consume a "soft" categorical (e.g. a mixture weighting), but not when the application genuinely requires a hard, discrete value (a discrete action in an RL environment, a quantized code). For that case the paper defines the Straight-Through (ST) Gumbel-Softmax estimator: on the forward pass, discretize y to a true one-hot vector via arg max (so the model actually consumes a discrete sample); on the backward pass, substitute the continuous y's gradient in place of the (undefined) gradient of the discretization step, i.e. approximate ∇θz ≈ ∇θy. This is the same biased trick behind Bengio et al.'s (2013) Straight-Through estimator for Bernoulli variables, just built on the smoother Gumbel-Softmax relaxation instead of a hard sigmoid — and it's what makes the method usable in the discrete-action settings (MADDPG's discrete communication channels among them) that motivated queuing this paper in the first place.

// what they found

The results

All experiments run on binarized MNIST, comparing Gumbel-Softmax and ST Gumbel-Softmax against Score-Function (SF/REINFORCE), DARN, MuProp, ST, and Slope-Annealed ST — each with both Bernoulli and categorical latent variables, and each estimator given its own learning-rate sweep selected on a validation set. On structured output prediction (predicting the bottom half of a digit from the top half, with a 392-200-200-392 stochastic binary network), Gumbel-Softmax reaches a test negative log-likelihood of 58.5 nats on Bernoulli variables and 59.0 nats on categorical variables (20 variables × 10 classes), beating every other single-sample estimator on both (next best: 58.7 nats Annealed-ST for Bernoulli, 61.1 nats Annealed-ST for categorical). On VAE training the gap widens: Gumbel-Softmax reaches a variational bound of 105.0 nats (Bernoulli) and 101.5 nats (categorical), versus the next-best baseline (MuProp) at 109.7 and 107.0 nats respectively — described in the paper as "drastically" outperforming the alternatives.

The paper's other headline number comes from applying the estimator to Kingma et al.'s (2014) semi-supervised VAE, where inference over a discrete class variable is normally done by marginalizing over every class — a cost that scales linearly with the number of classes, O(D + k(I + G)) per step. Sampling y directly with Gumbel-Softmax and backpropagating through the single sample instead costs O(D + I + G), independent of k. On MNIST with randomly generated labels, that turns into a measured 2× speedup at k=10 classes and 9.9× at k=100 classes (Figure 5a). On the actual semi-supervised classification task (100 labeled + 50,000 unlabeled examples), single-sample inference doesn't cost accuracy either:

Inference methodELBO (nats)Test accuracy
Marginalization (Kingma et al., 2014)−106.892.6%
Gumbel-Softmax−109.692.4%
ST Gumbel-Softmax−110.793.6%
// does it hold up?

The eval read

The temperature-variance trade-off the paper names in Section 2.1 isn't just a footnote — it's the whole reliability question for this estimator, and the paper's own resolution of it is empirical rather than principled: it anneals τ on a schedule and reports that "optimizing this objective in combination with temperature annealing still minimizes actual variational bounds on validation and test sets" (Section 4.2), which is a claim verified by observation, not a proof. In fact the paper is explicit that once τ is annealed away from a fixed value, "the minimization objective during training is no longer a variational bound" at all — the thing being optimized during most of training isn't exactly the quantity being reported at evaluation time, and the gap between them is never bounded, only shown empirically not to hurt on MNIST.

The annealing schedule itself is a hyperparameter surface the paper doesn't fully tame. For the VAE experiments it sweeps update interval N ∈ {500, 1000} and decay rate r ∈ {1e−5, 1e−4} and reports whichever validates best (Section 4.2) — that's a small grid here, but it's still a second hyperparameter search bolted onto every downstream use of the trick, and the paper's own structured-output-prediction experiments found annealing unnecessary and just fixed τ=1 (Section 4.1). So "how do I schedule τ" isn't answered once for the method — it's re-tuned per task, and the paper offers no guidance for tasks unlike its own MNIST setups. That matters a lot for exactly the use case this paper got queued for: MADDPG borrows Gumbel-Softmax for discrete communication actions in multi-agent RL, a setting with a moving, non-stationary training signal (other agents' policies are changing too) that looks nothing like binarized-MNIST density estimation, and where a bad temperature schedule plausibly interacts with training instability in ways this paper never tests.

All of the paper's own experiments are MNIST-scale: two small stochastic-network architectures (200-unit SBN, 200-unit VAE) and a semi-supervised classifier with 100 labeled examples. The RL application — discrete action spaces — is named twice as motivation (introduction, Section 2.2) but never once benchmarked in the paper; the generalization to reinforcement learning, and to larger or more structured discrete latent spaces than 20×10 categories, is asserted rather than demonstrated. The single-sample point estimates in Table 1 and Table 2 also carry no seed variance or error bars, and at least one baseline's own training curve (DARN, categorical VAE, Figure 4b) is visibly still drifting upward at 500k steps — a reminder that a single run's final number can flatter or hurt an estimator depending on where training happened to stop.

baselines

Fair and contemporaneous — SF/REINFORCE, DARN, MuProp, ST, and Slope-Annealed ST were the live single-sample gradient estimators for discrete stochastic nodes in late 2016, each given its own learning-rate sweep. VIMCO is explicitly and reasonably excluded as a multi-sample-objective estimator, not a hidden omission.

contamination

Not applicable in the usual sense — this is a method paper on binarized MNIST, a fixed public benchmark with no train/test leakage concern; the more relevant risk is scope leakage (validation-set hyperparameter selection, including the annealing schedule itself, tuned per task rather than fixed a priori).

what's missed

Whether the bias/variance trade-off and the annealing schedule actually transfer to non-MNIST, non-static settings — reinforcement learning with discrete actions and moving targets (the motivating use case) is never empirically tested in this paper at all, only proposed.

would raise confidence

Seed variance on the headline nats/accuracy numbers; an explicit ablation sweeping annealing schedules (rather than reporting only the validated-best one) to show how sensitive downstream performance really is; and at least one experiment on a genuinely discrete-action RL task rather than density estimation on MNIST.

// bottom line

The core idea — Gumbel noise plus a tempered softmax as a reparameterizable categorical — is simple, correct at the limit (τ→0 recovers the true categorical), and empirically strong on every task the paper actually tests. But "it works" here rides on an annealing schedule that's tuned per-experiment and never validated outside MNIST-scale problems, which is precisely the gap a downstream adopter like MADDPG has to paper over on its own when reusing the trick for discrete communication actions in a much harder, non-stationary multi-agent setting.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Gumbel-Softmax = add Gumbel noise to a categorical's logits, then softmax at temperature τ. It's a reparameterization trick for categorical variables in the same family as the VAE's z = μ + σ·N(0,1) — the sample becomes a differentiable function of the parameters and independent noise, so gradients flow through ordinary backprop instead of a score-function estimator.
  2. Temperature is the whole bias-variance story. τ→0 recovers true one-hot categorical samples but the gradient gets noisy; high τ is smooth but biased away from the discrete distribution being approximated. The paper anneals τ on a schedule tuned per experiment — there's no single schedule that's shown to transfer.
  3. The Straight-Through variant is what makes it usable when a genuinely discrete value is required (a hard action, a quantized code) — discretize on the forward pass, backprop through the soft relaxation on the backward pass. This is the exact mechanism MADDPG borrows to make discrete communication actions differentiable in multi-agent RL, well outside the MNIST-scale settings this paper actually tests.
  4. Independently co-discovered as the "Concrete distribution" (Maddison, Mnih & Teh, 2016) — the identical relaxation, published essentially simultaneously under a different name, which is part of why the trick propagated so fast through VAEs, RL, and beyond.
